skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Route AI avatar, talking-head, and lip-sync video requests to the right RunComfy model and CLI command.
skill.summary: Create talking-head and lip-sync videos through RunComfy model routes.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many command, network, filesystem, and blocker patterns, but review shows most command hits are Markdown examples for the declared RunComfy CLI workflow. The skill is not blocked because no malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but it should publish with a medium-risk warning for external model execution, user media URLs, token storage, and synthetic likeness misuse.

medium_findings.title: External RunComfy CLI execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill is designed to run the RunComfy CLI and submit model jobs. This is legitimate for the skill purpose and constrained by allowed-tools, but it still executes an external command and sends generation requests outside the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and repeated invoke examples confirm real CLI use. The risk is bounded because the skill names only RunComfy commands rather than arbitrary shell execution.

medium_findings.title: Outbound network use with user media URLs
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill requires remote model APIs and user-provided image, audio, and reference URLs. This can expose likeness, voice, and prompt data to RunComfy or user-hosted asset endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples and security notes explicitly rely on remote asset URLs and RunComfy network endpoints. This is inherent to the workflow, not an accidental scanner artifact.

medium_findings.title: Credential storage in hidden configuration file
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents that login stores an API token under a hidden user configuration path. It states mode 0600 and offers an environment variable option, so this is a credential handling concern rather than evidence of theft.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and permission mode are explicitly documented. The same line also describes a mitigation, which lowers severity from high to medium.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use synthetic likeness workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill can generate videos from a portrait and voice audio, which can enable non-consensual impersonation. The skill includes consent and refusal guidance, but the capability remains sensitive.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic risk is directly tied to avatar and voice-driven generation. The file acknowledges the consent issue, so the concern is clear but partially mitigated.

low_findings.title: Static weak-cryptography findings are false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported blocker locations do not show cryptographic operations. They describe model routing, audio fields, prompting guidance, and an exit-code table, so there is no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no hash, cipher, random number, or signature logic. The scanner appears to have matched unrelated text.

low_findings.title: Static system-reconnaissance findings are false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited lines are model selection advice and do not inspect host state, enumerate files, collect environment details, or run discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is plain documentation that says when to avoid specific model routes. There is no evidence of host reconnaissance behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are mostly documentation and service endpoints
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Most URL findings point to RunComfy documentation, model pages, and example CDN placeholders. They are expected for this integration, but users should still understand that real runs use remote services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible, descriptive, and aligned with the skill purpose. Some network risk remains because the workflow depends on external endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell command examples for external generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides multiple runcomfy command examples. They are not arbitrary shell payloads, but they should only run with user-approved inputs and the declared Bash(runcomfy *) scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present and intentional. It is dangerous only if the executing agent ignores the documented input boundary or expands command scope.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent API token location
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented login flow writes a RunComfy token to a hidden configuration file. This is normal CLI behavior but needs secure permissions and user awareness.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact credential storage behavior is documented. The stated file mode is a mitigation, not proof that every environment enforces it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Untrusted reference media can influence generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill warns that reference image and audio URLs are untrusted and can influence generated output. This is an indirect prompt injection and content integrity risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly identifies third-party reference assets as untrusted. The mitigation is useful, but the risk remains part of the workflow.

content.value_statement: Teams need fast talking-head videos without manually comparing many video models. This skill chooses a RunComfy avatar route and prepares the matching CLI command.
actual_capabilities: Selects between OmniHuman, Wan, HappyHorse, Seedance, and Wan Animate routes.
Builds RunComfy CLI invocations for portrait, audio, and reference media inputs.
Explains when to use audio-driven lip sync versus generated in-pass audio.
Provides prompting patterns for product demos, avatars, mascots, and cinematic monologues.
Documents model limits, output handling, exit codes, and privacy considerations.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and access to RunComfy model endpoints.
Requires users to provide valid rights for likeness, voice, and reference media.
Does not create or host source images, audio files, or CDN URLs by itself.
Model availability, pricing, and output quality depend on RunComfy services.

use_cases.title: Create product spokespeople
use_cases.description: Generate short avatar videos from a portrait and voiceover for demos, ads, or onboarding clips.

use_cases.title: Localize presenter videos
use_cases.description: Reuse one approved portrait with different language audio tracks to produce dubbed variants.

use_cases.title: Animate character content
use_cases.description: Choose a route for stylized characters, mascots, or cinematic scenes with reference media.

prompt_templates.title: Basic portrait speaker
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner has one portrait and one clean voiceover file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a talking-head video from this portrait URL and this voiceover URL. Use the simplest RunComfy route and explain the needed inputs.

prompt_templates.title: Script to avatar clip
prompt_templates.scenario: The user has a script but no recorded audio.
prompt_templates.prompt: Make an avatar video from this short script without an audio file. Choose the model route and format the spoken line clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Brand demo with specific voice
prompt_templates.scenario: The user needs accurate lip sync to an existing audio file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a vertical product demo with this portrait and this approved voiceover. Choose between OmniHuman and Wan based on scene control.

prompt_templates.title: Cinematic reference workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: An advanced user has subject, scene, and audio references.
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a cinematic avatar monologue using these reference images and audio. Pick the best RunComfy model and include prompt guidance.

output_examples.input: I have a presenter portrait and an MP3 voiceover for a product launch.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends OmniHuman for a direct portrait-plus-audio workflow and returns the RunComfy command shape, required inputs, and output folder guidance.

output_examples.input: I only have a two-sentence script and need a vertical social clip.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends HappyHorse because no external audio exists, then formats the spoken line and video settings for a short vertical clip.

output_examples.input: I want a stylized mascot to speak using my approved audio file.
output_examples.output: The skill routes to Wan 2-2 Animate and explains how to provide the character image, audio URL, and output directory.

best_practices: Use only portraits, voices, and reference media that you own or have permission to use.
Provide clean speech audio without music when accurate lip sync matters.
Keep scripts short and create multiple clips for longer videos.
anti_patterns: Do not create videos of real people without documented consent.
Do not paste untrusted prompts or URLs into shell commands outside the intended CLI input.
Do not use low-quality mixed audio when precise mouth sync is required.

faq.question: Does this skill generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. It prepares RunComfy CLI requests that run on RunComfy model services.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it work without an audio file?
faq.answer: Yes. It can route script-only requests to a model that generates speech during video creation.

faq.question: Can it use a specific voiceover file?
faq.answer: Yes. Several routes accept an audio URL for mouth sync or reference audio.

faq.question: What consent is required?
faq.answer: Users need rights to the subject likeness, voice audio, and any reference media.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill runs an external CLI, sends media to remote services, and stores a service token.
